 {"id":"wa-1qz1.4","title":"Conformal prediction for resource forecasting","description":"## Goal
Implement split conformal prediction for FrankenTerm resource metrics, providing distribution-free prediction intervals with formal coverage guarantees for RSS, CPU, and I/O.

## Background & Motivation
FrankenTerm currently has no resource forecasting. The mux server can consume 76GB RSS over 23 days with no warning. By adding conformal prediction, we get mathematically guaranteed prediction intervals:
"With 95% probability, RSS will be between 52GB and 68GB in 2 hours."

Unlike parametric forecasting (which assumes a distribution), conformal prediction is DISTRIBUTION-FREE — it provides valid coverage guarantees with NO assumptions about the data generating process.

## Technical Design

### Split Conformal Prediction
1. Collect calibration set: recent resource observations {(x₁,y₁), ..., (xₙ,yₙ)}
   - xᵢ = feature vector (current RSS, pane_count, output_rate, uptime, hour_of_day)
   - yᵢ = actual RSS value at horizon (e.g., 30 min later)

2. Train simple point predictor ŷ = f(x) on training split (70% of data)

3. Compute nonconformity scores on calibration split (30%):
   sᵢ = |yᵢ - f(xᵢ)| for each calibration point

4. For new prediction at coverage level 1-α:
   - Sort calibration scores: s₍₁₎ ≤ s₍₂₎ ≤ ... ≤ s₍ₙ₎
   - Find q = ⌈(1-α)(n+1)⌉-th score
   - Prediction interval: [f(x_new) - q, f(x_new) + q]

### Coverage Guarantee (Theorem)
For exchangeable data, conformal prediction provides:
  P(y_new ∈ C(x_new)) ≥ 1 - α

This holds regardless of the underlying distribution. No parametric assumptions needed.

### Point Predictor
Use exponential smoothing with trend (Holt's method):
- Level: ℓₜ = α×yₜ + (1-α)(ℓₜ₋₁ + bₜ₋₁)
- Trend: bₜ = β(ℓₜ - ℓₜ₋₁) + (1-β)bₜ₋₁
- Forecast: ŷₜ₊ₕ = ℓₜ + h×bₜ

Holt's method is lightweight (O(1) per update, O(1) memory) and captures trends well.

### Multiple Forecasting Horizons
Generate intervals for: 30min, 1h, 2h, 4h, 8h
Each horizon has its own calibration set and nonconformity scores.

### Metrics Forecasted
- RSS (mux server memory): Primary concern
- CPU utilization (per-pane and aggregate)
- Disk I/O (write rate for SQLite storage)
- Capture queue depth (backpressure indicator)

### Alert Integration
When prediction interval upper bound exceeds alarm threshold:
- RSS upper bound > 80% of available memory → trigger proactive snapshot
- CPU upper bound > 90% → suggest reducing pane count
- Queue depth upper bound > 80% capacity → pre-emptive backpressure

### Galaxy-Brain Transparency
```
┌─ Conformal Resource Forecast ────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Method: Split Conformal Prediction (coverage=95%, n_cal=200)     │
│                                                                   │
│ RSS Forecast:                                                     │
│   Now:  48.2 GB                                                   │
│   +30m: [49.1, 52.3] GB  (point: 50.7 GB)                       │
│   +1h:  [50.3, 56.8] GB  (point: 53.5 GB)                       │
│   +4h:  [54.1, 71.2] GB  (point: 62.7 GB)  ⚠️ > 80% memory     │
│                                                                   │
│ Coverage guarantee: P(actual ∈ interval) ≥ 0.95                  │
│ (Distribution-free — no parametric assumptions)                   │
│                                                                   │
│ 💡 RSS trending up. Recommend proactive snapshot within 2 hours. │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation Location
- New file: crates/wa-core/src/conformal.rs
- Integration: metrics.rs feeds observations, survival.rs consumes forecasts
- Storage: resource_observations table for calibration data

## Existing Code References
- metrics.rs: MetricsSnapshot (source of resource observations)
- environment.rs: detect_memory_mb() (available memory baseline)
- watchdog.rs: health check interval (data collection cadence)

## Configuration
```toml
[forecasting]
enabled = true
coverage = 0.95                   # 95% coverage guarantee
calibration_window_hours = 72     # Use last 72h for calibration
horizons_minutes = [30, 60, 120, 240, 480]
rss_alarm_fraction = 0.80         # Alert at 80% of available
collection_interval_secs = 30     # How often to record observations
```

## Dependencies
- Needs telemetry pipeline for RSS/CPU/I/O collection
- Feeds into survival model (provides resource trajectory forecasts)
- Integrates with session persistence for proactive save triggers

## Test Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
- `conformal_holt_update`: Single Holt's method update (level + trend) must complete in <100ns.
- `conformal_interval_computation`: Computing a prediction interval (sort calibration scores, find quantile) for n_cal=200 must complete in <10μs.
- `conformal_multi_horizon_forecast`: Computing prediction intervals for all 5 horizons must complete in <50μs.
- `conformal_calibration_refresh`: Re-computing calibration scores from 200 observation pairs must complete in <500μs.
- Add `[[bench]] name = "conformal" harness = false` to Cargo.toml; use criterion::BenchmarkGroup for the above targets.

### Proptest Specifications
- `proptest_coverage_guarantee`: For any sequence of N exchangeable observations (N in 50..500) drawn from an arbitrary distribution (proptest generates distribution parameters), the empirical coverage of the conformal interval on held-out test points must be >= (1-α) - 1/√N (allowing for finite-sample deviation).
- `proptest_interval_width_monotonicity`: For fixed data, increasing coverage (1-α) must always produce wider or equal prediction intervals. Generate α values in 0.01..0.50, verify interval_width(α₁) >= interval_width(α₂) when α₁ < α₂.
- `proptest_holt_numerical_stability`: For any sequence of observations (including extreme values up to f64::MAX/2), Holt's method must not produce NaN or Inf. Generate 100 random observations with values in -1e15..1e15.
- `proptest_calibration_score_ordering`: Nonconformity scores must always be non-negative. Generate random predictions and actuals, verify all scores >= 0.

## Cross-References
- **wa-2ahu0** (Memory pressure engine): Conformal RSS forecasts feed directly into the memory pressure engine — when the upper bound of the RSS prediction interval exceeds the configured memory threshold, the pressure engine can proactively trigger graduated memory relief (snapshot + GC) before actual pressure materializes. The conformal interval width also informs the pressure engine's confidence in its response timing.
- **wa-8eoug** (CPU pressure scheduling): Conformal CPU utilization forecasts enable the CPU pressure scheduler to anticipate load spikes and pre-emptively adjust pane scheduling priorities. The multi-horizon forecasts (especially 30min and 1h) provide the lead time needed for smooth priority transitions rather than reactive throttling.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Split conformal prediction with Holt's method point predictor
- Formal coverage guarantee validated: actual coverage ≥ 1-α on held-out data
- Multiple horizon forecasts (30m to 8h)
- Alert integration when upper bounds exceed thresholds
- Galaxy-brain card via wa robot API
- Lightweight: O(1) per update, O(n_calibration) for interval computation
- Unit tests: synthetic data with known coverage, edge cases (constant, trending, bursty)
- Integration test: simulate RSS growth, verify alert fires before OOM
- Criterion benchmarks meeting latency targets
- Proptest suites for coverage guarantees and numerical stability","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"DarkGate","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:23:55.146555Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:36:55.044514-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T01:36:55.044514-05:00","close_reason":"Conformal prediction module complete: HoltPredictor, CalibrationSet, MetricForecaster, ConformalForecaster with 27 tests (4 proptest suites). All clippy lints fixed.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.4","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:23:55.146555Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.4","depends_on_id":"wa-3kxe.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:51.848918Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1qz1.5","title":"Bayesian evidence ledger for pane classification","description":"## Goal
Replace wa-3bin's EWMA+threshold pane classification with full Bayesian posterior inference over pane states, providing evidence ledgers that show exactly WHY each classification decision was made.

## Background & Motivation
wa-3bin (Smart Pane Priority) currently uses EWMA with exponential decay for output rate tracking and threshold-based classification. This works but:
1. Thresholds are arbitrary (rate > 10.0 → High, else Medium)
2. No confidence measure (is it 51% or 99% sure it's "Active"?)
3. Not explainable (WHY was this pane classified this way?)
4. Doesn't combine evidence from multiple signals formally

Bayesian classification solves all four by maintaining full posterior distributions and evidence ledgers.

## Technical Design

### State Space
S = {Active, Thinking, Idle, RateLimited, Error, Stuck, Background}

### Evidence Sources (per pane)
Each evidence source contributes a log-likelihood ratio:
1. Output rate (from capture pipeline): lines/sec
2. Entropy of recent output (from BOCPD feature computation)
3. Pattern detections (from patterns.rs): error, rate_limit, thinking, etc.
4. Time since last output (from tailer.rs)
5. Scrollback growth rate (from storage.rs)

### Posterior Update
For each pane, maintain log-posterior:
  log P(S=s|evidence) = log P(S=s) + Σᵢ log P(evidenceᵢ|S=s)

Prior P(S=s) is the stationary distribution learned from historical data.

Likelihoods P(evidenceᵢ|S=s) are learned from labeled observations:
- P(output_rate=12.3|Active) from historical Active-labeled panes
- P(output_rate=12.3|Idle) from historical Idle-labeled panes

### Evidence Ledger
For each classification, record:
```json
{
  "pane_id": 7,
  "classification": "Stuck",
  "posterior": {"Active": 0.02, "Thinking": 0.05, "Idle": 0.08, "Stuck": 0.82, ...},
  "evidence_ledger": [
    {"source": "output_rate", "value": 0.3, "log_lr_stuck_vs_active": 3.2, "contribution": "strong_for_stuck"},
    {"source": "entropy", "value": 1.1, "log_lr_stuck_vs_active": 2.8, "contribution": "strong_for_stuck"},
    {"source": "unique_ratio", "value": 0.05, "log_lr_stuck_vs_active": 4.1, "contribution": "very_strong_for_stuck"},
    {"source": "pattern_detection", "value": "none", "log_lr_stuck_vs_active": 0.5, "contribution": "weak_for_stuck"}
  ],
  "bayes_factor_vs_next_best": 15.3,
  "timestamp": 1738000000
}
```

### Bayes Factor Interpretation
- BF < 3: Barely worth mentioning
- 3 < BF < 10: Substantial evidence
- 10 < BF < 30: Strong evidence
- 30 < BF < 100: Very strong evidence
- BF > 100: Decisive

### Learning from Feedback
When user manually overrides a classification (via wa robot priority set):
- Treat as labeled observation
- Update likelihood parameters via conjugate update
- System gets smarter over time

### Galaxy-Brain Transparency
```
┌─ Pane 7 Classification ─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ log P(Stuck|evidence) = -0.20 → P = 0.82                       │
│ log P(Active|evidence) = -3.91 → P = 0.02                      │
│                                                                  │
│ Evidence ledger:                                                 │
│   output_rate=0.3/s:  log LR(Stuck/Active) = +3.2  ███████░    │
│   entropy=1.1 bits:   log LR(Stuck/Active) = +2.8  ██████░░    │
│   unique_ratio=0.05:  log LR(Stuck/Active) = +4.1  █████████   │
│   no_pattern_match:   log LR(Stuck/Active) = +0.5  ██░░░░░░    │
│                                                                  │
│ Bayes factor vs Active: 15.3 (strong evidence)                  │
│                                                                  │
│ 💡 Repetitive low-entropy output with very low unique ratio.    │
│    Agent likely stuck in a loop.                                │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Implementation Location
- Modify: crates/wa-core/src/priority.rs (replace EWMA with Bayesian model)
- New: evidence ledger data structures in priority.rs
- Integration: wa robot priorities shows evidence ledgers

## Existing Code References
- wa-3bin design: EWMA OutputRateTracker (being replaced)
- patterns.rs: Detection events (evidence source)
- tailer.rs: PaneTailer last_output tracking (evidence source)
- ingest.rs: capture cycle data (feature computation)

## Configuration
```toml
[classification]
mode = "bayesian"              # "bayesian" | "ewma" (fallback)
prior_update_interval_secs = 300
min_observations = 10          # Before classification starts
bayes_factor_threshold = 3.0   # Minimum for confident classification
```

## Dependencies
- Enhanced by BOCPD (provides entropy/unique_ratio features)
- Feeds into VOI scheduler (provides importance weight W(i))
- Related to wa-3bin (this is the principled replacement)

## Test Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
- `bayesian_single_evidence_update`: Single evidence update (one source, one pane, 7 states) must complete in <10us (target: <5us typical). Benchmark with log-posterior update across all 7 states.
- `bayesian_full_classification`: Full classification cycle (5 evidence sources, posterior update, Bayes factor computation, evidence ledger construction) must complete in <50us per pane.
- `bayesian_batch_100_panes`: Classify 100 panes with full evidence ledgers must complete in <5ms.
- `bayesian_feedback_update`: Conjugate prior update from user feedback must complete in <20us.
- Add `[[bench]] name = "bayesian_classifier" harness = false` to Cargo.toml; use criterion::BenchmarkGroup for the above targets.

### Proptest Specifications
- `proptest_posterior_normalization`: After any sequence of evidence updates (generate random log-likelihood ratios in -10.0..10.0 for 7 states, 1-20 evidence sources), the posterior must sum to 1.0 (within f64 epsilon after exp-normalize).
- `proptest_bayes_factor_consistency`: The Bayes factor between top two states must equal exp(log_posterior_1 - log_posterior_2). Generate random posteriors, verify BF matches.
- `proptest_evidence_ledger_completeness`: For any set of evidence sources, the evidence ledger must contain exactly one entry per source, and the sum of log-LR contributions must equal the total log-posterior shift. Generate 1-10 random evidence updates.
- `proptest_monotone_evidence`: Adding evidence that favors state S must never decrease P(S). Generate a random prior and a single evidence update with positive log-LR for state S, verify P(S) increases.
- `proptest_conjugate_update_convergence`: After N feedback observations (N in 5..100) all labeled as state S, the prior for state S must increase monotonically.

## Cross-References
- **wa-3bin** (Smart pane priority): This bead is the principled Bayesian replacement for wa-3bin's EWMA+threshold classification. The evidence ledger provides the explainability that wa-3bin's simple thresholds lack. wa-3bin's priority tiers (High/Medium/Low/Background) map to posterior-weighted importance scores that feed into the VOI scheduler (wa-1qz1.3).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Full posterior over 7 pane states per pane
- Evidence ledger for every classification decision
- Bayes factors comparing top two states
- Online learning from user feedback (manual overrides)
- Galaxy-brain card via wa robot API
- Falls back to EWMA when insufficient data
- Unit tests: synthetic evidence sequences with known correct classifications
- Integration test: classify panes in recorded agent workload trace
- Criterion benchmarks meeting latency targets (<10us per update)
- Proptest suites for Bayesian consistency and evidence ledger correctness","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"WildBeaver","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:00.951918Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:50:36.179827-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T01:50:36.179827-05:00","close_reason":"Bayesian evidence ledger implemented with 35 passing tests","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.5","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:00.951918Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.5","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:44.625246Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.5","depends_on_id":"wa-3bin","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:25:08.141993Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1qz1.6","title":"Continuous backpressure severity function","description":"## Goal
Replace the 4-tier discrete backpressure FSM with a continuous severity function, enabling smooth throttling curves instead of step functions, with exponential smoothing for hysteresis.

## Background & Motivation
backpressure.rs (657 lines) uses a 4-tier FSM (Green/Yellow/Red/Black) with hardcoded thresholds (50%/75% capture, 60%/80% write). Transitions are discrete steps that cause abrupt behavior changes:
- Going from Green to Yellow instantly doubles idle polling intervals
- Going from Yellow to Red instantly pauses 50% of panes
- Fixed 2000ms hysteresis prevents flapping but can't adapt to workload patterns

A continuous severity function provides smooth transitions where throttling intensity is proportional to load.

## Technical Design

### Severity Function
  s(t) = sigmoid(k * (q(t) - theta))

Where:
- q(t) = exponentially smoothed queue ratio (capture or write, whichever is higher)
- theta = center threshold (default 0.60, where severity = 0.5)
- k = steepness parameter (default 8.0, controls how fast severity rises)
- sigmoid(x) = 1 / (1 + exp(-x))

### Exponential Smoothing (replaces fixed hysteresis)
  q_smooth(t) = alpha * q_raw(t) + (1-alpha) * q_smooth(t-1)

Where alpha = 2/(N+1), N = smoothing window in samples (default N=10, so alpha is approximately 0.18)

This provides natural hysteresis: rapid load increases are smoothed, preventing oscillation without a fixed delay.

### Throttling Actions (continuous)
Instead of discrete tier actions, each action has a continuous intensity:
- Polling backoff multiplier: 1.0 + 3.0 * s(t)  (range: 1x to 4x)
- Pane skip fraction: 0.5 * s(t)^2  (range: 0% to 50%, quadratic to avoid premature shedding)
- Pattern detection skip: 0.25 * s(t)  (range: 0% to 25%)
- Buffer limit: max_segments * (1 - 0.8 * s(t))  (range: 100% to 20% of normal)

### Backward Compatibility
The existing BackpressureTier enum is preserved for external consumers:
```rust
impl ContinuousBackpressure {
    pub fn equivalent_tier(&self) -> BackpressureTier {
        match self.severity() {
            s if s < 0.25 => Green,
            s if s < 0.60 => Yellow,
            s if s < 0.85 => Red,
            _ => Black,
        }
    }
}
```

### macOS Platform Fallback for Pressure Data
The severity function ingests system pressure metrics to weight the queue ratio. On Linux, this reads /proc/pressure/* (PSI - Pressure Stall Information) for CPU, memory, and I/O pressure. On macOS, PSI is not available. The implementation must provide a platform abstraction:

- **Linux**: Read /proc/pressure/cpu, /proc/pressure/memory, /proc/pressure/io to obtain avg10/avg60/avg300 stall percentages. Parse the "some" and "full" lines.
- **macOS**: Use the Mach host_statistics64() API with HOST_VM_INFO64_COUNT to obtain vm_statistics64 data. Derive memory pressure from (active + wired) / total physical memory. For CPU pressure, use host_processor_info() to compute aggregate CPU utilization. For I/O pressure, use IOKit's IOStatisticsGetReport or fall back to disk activity heuristics from host_statistics (pageins/pageouts rate).
- **Abstraction layer**: Define a `SystemPressure` trait with a single `fn read_pressure() -> PressureSnapshot` method. Implement `LinuxPsiPressure` (reads /proc/pressure/*) and `MacosMachPressure` (uses vm_statistics64/host_statistics64). Select implementation at compile time via `#[cfg(target_os)]`.
- **PressureSnapshot struct**: Contains cpu_pressure (0.0-1.0), memory_pressure (0.0-1.0), io_pressure (0.0-1.0), normalized to a common scale regardless of platform.

### Implementation Location
- Modify: crates/wa-core/src/backpressure.rs (add continuous model alongside discrete)
- New: crates/wa-core/src/pressure.rs (platform-abstracted pressure reading)
- Integration: tailer.rs reads severity directly instead of tier enum
- Backward compat: existing tier consumers get mapped tier

## Existing Code References
- backpressure.rs: BackpressureConfig, BackpressureTier, BackpressureState
- tailer.rs: uses BackpressureTier for polling adjustment
- degradation.rs: uses BackpressureTier for load shedding decisions

## Configuration
```toml
[backpressure]
mode = "continuous"           # "continuous" | "discrete" (current FSM)
center_threshold = 0.60       # Queue ratio where severity = 0.5
steepness = 8.0               # Sigmoid steepness
smoothing_window = 10         # EMA window in samples
```

## Dependencies
None -- standalone enhancement to backpressure.rs.

## Test Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
- `severity_computation`: Single severity function evaluation (sigmoid + EMA update) must complete in <50ns.
- `severity_with_pressure_read`: Severity computation including platform pressure read must complete in <10us (macOS vm_statistics64 call is typically <5us).
- `throttle_action_computation`: Computing all 4 throttle action intensities from severity must complete in <100ns.
- `tier_mapping`: Converting continuous severity to BackpressureTier must complete in <10ns.
- Add `[[bench]] name = "backpressure_continuous" harness = false` to Cargo.toml; use criterion::BenchmarkGroup for the above targets.

### Proptest Specifications
- `proptest_severity_monotonicity`: For any two queue ratios q1 < q2 (both in 0.0..1.0), severity(q1) <= severity(q2). The sigmoid is monotonically increasing, so this must always hold regardless of steepness or center_threshold parameters.
- `proptest_severity_continuity`: For any queue ratio q and small delta (delta in 0.0001..0.01), |severity(q+delta) - severity(q)| < delta * k (Lipschitz bound where k is the steepness parameter). This ensures no discontinuities.
- `proptest_severity_range`: For any queue ratio q in 0.0..1.0 and any valid configuration (center_threshold in 0.1..0.9, steepness in 1.0..20.0), severity must be in [0.0, 1.0].
- `proptest_ema_convergence`: After N identical observations (N in 10..100) of value v, the smoothed value must converge to v within 0.01. Generate random v in 0.0..1.0.
- `proptest_tier_mapping_consistency`: Tier mappings must be ordered: severity < 0.25 always maps to Green, and increasing severity never maps to a lower tier.

## Cross-References
- **wa-2ahu0** (Memory pressure engine): The continuous severity function provides the fine-grained pressure signal that wa-2ahu0's memory pressure engine consumes. Rather than discrete tier transitions, wa-2ahu0 can use the raw severity value (0.0-1.0) to proportionally scale its memory relief actions (GC intensity, snapshot urgency).
- **wa-8eoug** (CPU pressure scheduling): The CPU pressure component of the PressureSnapshot (from the macOS/Linux abstraction layer) feeds directly into wa-8eoug's scheduling decisions. The continuous nature avoids the scheduling oscillations that discrete tier transitions would cause.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Continuous severity function s(t) in [0, 1]
- Exponential smoothing for natural hysteresis
- Proportional throttling actions (no step changes)
- Backward-compatible tier mapping for existing consumers
- Platform-abstracted pressure reading (Linux PSI, macOS vm_statistics64/host_statistics64)
- Unit tests: verify smoothing, severity computation, action proportionality
- Integration test: simulate load spike, verify smooth throttling curve
- Criterion benchmarks meeting latency targets
- Proptest suites for monotonicity, continuity, and range invariants","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"WildBeaver","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:01.922332Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:54:46.132582-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T01:54:46.132582-05:00","close_reason":"Continuous backpressure severity function implemented: sigmoid severity, EMA smoothing, proportional throttle actions, backward-compatible tier mapping. 27 unit tests + 7 proptest properties all passing.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.6","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:01.922332Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.6","depends_on_id":"wa-3cyp","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:25:08.277099Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
-{"id":"wa-1qz1.7","title":"Adaptive watchdog thresholds via Kalman filter","description":"## Goal
Replace fixed heartbeat thresholds in watchdog.rs with Kalman-filtered adaptive thresholds that learn normal inter-heartbeat intervals and detect anomalies relative to observed behavior.

## Background & Motivation
watchdog.rs uses fixed thresholds: capture is stale after 5s, discovery after 15s, persistence after 30s, maintenance after 120s. These are reasonable defaults but:
1. Don't adapt to system load (under pressure, heartbeats naturally slow)
2. Can't distinguish "slightly slow" from "completely hung"
3. False alarms when the system is legitimately loaded

A Kalman filter estimates the true heartbeat interval and its variance, allowing the watchdog to set thresholds at mu + k*sigma (k standard deviations above normal).

## Technical Design

### Per-Component Kalman State
For each component (discovery, capture, persistence, maintenance):
- State: x = [interval, drift]^T (interval between heartbeats + trend)
- Measurement: z = actual observed interval
- Process noise Q: accounts for natural variation
- Measurement noise R: accounts for timing jitter

### Kalman Update (per heartbeat)
```
Predict:
  x_hat = F * x + B * u
  P_hat = F * P * F^T + Q

Update:
  K = P_hat * H^T * (H * P_hat * H^T + R)^-1
  x = x_hat + K * (z - H * x_hat)
  P = (I - K * H) * P_hat
```

For 1D (interval only, no drift): simplifies to scalar Kalman filter.

### Adaptive Threshold
  threshold(t) = x(t) + k * sqrt(P(t))

Where k controls sensitivity:
- k=2: ~95% of normal heartbeats within threshold (moderate sensitivity)
- k=3: ~99.7% within threshold (low false alarm rate)
- k=4: very conservative

### Health Status (refined)
Instead of binary Healthy/Degraded/Critical:
- z-score = (observed_interval - x(t)) / sqrt(P(t))
- z < 2: Healthy
- 2 <= z < 3: Degraded (unusual but plausible)
- 3 <= z < 5: Critical (very unlikely to be normal)
- z >= 5: Hung (essentially certain anomaly)

### Implementation Location
- Modify: crates/wa-core/src/watchdog.rs (add Kalman filter alongside fixed thresholds)
- Per-component KalmanState stored in HeartbeatRegistry

## Existing Code References
- watchdog.rs: HeartbeatRegistry, WatchdogConfig, HealthStatus
- 4 components: discovery (15s), capture (5s), persistence (30s), maintenance (120s)

## Configuration
```toml
[watchdog]
mode = "adaptive"             # "adaptive" | "fixed" (current)
sensitivity_k = 3.0           # Standard deviations for threshold
process_noise = 0.1           # Kalman Q (higher = more adaptive)
measurement_noise = 0.5       # Kalman R (higher = smoother estimates)
min_observations = 5          # Before switching from fixed to adaptive
```

## Dependencies
None -- standalone enhancement to watchdog.rs.

## Test Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
- `kalman_scalar_update`: Single scalar Kalman filter predict+update cycle must complete in <100ns.
- `kalman_threshold_computation`: Adaptive threshold computation (mu + k*sigma) must complete in <50ns.
- `kalman_zscore_health_status`: z-score computation and health status classification must complete in <50ns.
- `kalman_batch_4_components`: Update all 4 component Kalman filters (discovery, capture, persistence, maintenance) must complete in <500ns.
- Add `[[bench]] name = "kalman_watchdog" harness = false` to Cargo.toml; use criterion::BenchmarkGroup for the above targets.

### Proptest Specifications
- `proptest_kalman_convergence`: For a stationary heartbeat interval (constant true interval in 1.0..120.0 with Gaussian noise sigma in 0.1..5.0), after N observations (N in 20..200), the Kalman estimate must be within 2*sigma/sqrt(N) of the true interval. Generate random true intervals and noise levels.
- `proptest_variance_positive_definite`: After any sequence of observations (values in 0.1..300.0, length 1..500), the Kalman variance P must remain strictly positive (P > 0). This is essential for numerical stability.
- `proptest_threshold_above_estimate`: The adaptive threshold must always be >= the Kalman estimate (since k >= 0). For any k in 0.0..10.0 and any filter state, verify threshold >= estimate.

+{"id":"wa-1qz1.7","title":"Adaptive watchdog thresholds via Kalman filter","description":"## Goal
Replace fixed heartbeat thresholds in watchdog.rs with Kalman-filtered adaptive thresholds that learn normal inter-heartbeat intervals and detect anomalies relative to observed behavior.

## Background & Motivation
watchdog.rs uses fixed thresholds: capture is stale after 5s, discovery after 15s, persistence after 30s, maintenance after 120s. These are reasonable defaults but:
1. Don't adapt to system load (under pressure, heartbeats naturally slow)
2. Can't distinguish "slightly slow" from "completely hung"
3. False alarms when the system is legitimately loaded

A Kalman filter estimates the true heartbeat interval and its variance, allowing the watchdog to set thresholds at mu + k*sigma (k standard deviations above normal).

## Technical Design

### Per-Component Kalman State
For each component (discovery, capture, persistence, maintenance):
- State: x = [interval, drift]^T (interval between heartbeats + trend)
- Measurement: z = actual observed interval
- Process noise Q: accounts for natural variation
- Measurement noise R: accounts for timing jitter

### Kalman Update (per heartbeat)
```
Predict:
  x_hat = F * x + B * u
  P_hat = F * P * F^T + Q

Update:
  K = P_hat * H^T * (H * P_hat * H^T + R)^-1
  x = x_hat + K * (z - H * x_hat)
  P = (I - K * H) * P_hat
```

For 1D (interval only, no drift): simplifies to scalar Kalman filter.

### Adaptive Threshold
  threshold(t) = x(t) + k * sqrt(P(t))

Where k controls sensitivity:
- k=2: ~95% of normal heartbeats within threshold (moderate sensitivity)
- k=3: ~99.7% within threshold (low false alarm rate)
- k=4: very conservative

### Health Status (refined)
Instead of binary Healthy/Degraded/Critical:
- z-score = (observed_interval - x(t)) / sqrt(P(t))
- z < 2: Healthy
- 2 <= z < 3: Degraded (unusual but plausible)
- 3 <= z < 5: Critical (very unlikely to be normal)
- z >= 5: Hung (essentially certain anomaly)

### Implementation Location
- Modify: crates/wa-core/src/watchdog.rs (add Kalman filter alongside fixed thresholds)
- Per-component KalmanState stored in HeartbeatRegistry

## Existing Code References
- watchdog.rs: HeartbeatRegistry, WatchdogConfig, HealthStatus
- 4 components: discovery (15s), capture (5s), persistence (30s), maintenance (120s)

## Configuration
```toml
[watchdog]
mode = "adaptive"             # "adaptive" | "fixed" (current)
sensitivity_k = 3.0           # Standard deviations for threshold
process_noise = 0.1           # Kalman Q (higher = more adaptive)
measurement_noise = 0.5       # Kalman R (higher = smoother estimates)
min_observations = 5          # Before switching from fixed to adaptive
```

## Dependencies
None -- standalone enhancement to watchdog.rs.

## Test Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
- `kalman_scalar_update`: Single scalar Kalman filter predict+update cycle must complete in <100ns.
- `kalman_threshold_computation`: Adaptive threshold computation (mu + k*sigma) must complete in <50ns.
- `kalman_zscore_health_status`: z-score computation and health status classification must complete in <50ns.
- `kalman_batch_4_components`: Update all 4 component Kalman filters (discovery, capture, persistence, maintenance) must complete in <500ns.
- Add `[[bench]] name = "kalman_watchdog" harness = false` to Cargo.toml; use criterion::BenchmarkGroup for the above targets.

### Proptest Specifications
- `proptest_kalman_convergence`: For a stationary heartbeat interval (constant true interval in 1.0..120.0 with Gaussian noise sigma in 0.1..5.0), after N observations (N in 20..200), the Kalman estimate must be within 2*sigma/sqrt(N) of the true interval. Generate random true intervals and noise levels.
- `proptest_variance_positive_definite`: After any sequence of observations (values in 0.1..300.0, length 1..500), the Kalman variance P must remain strictly positive (P > 0). This is essential for numerical stability.
- `proptest_threshold_above_estimate`: The adaptive threshold must always be >= the Kalman estimate (since k >= 0). For any k in 0.0..10.0 and any filter state, verify threshold >= estimate.
- `proptest_zscore_ordering`: For observations sorted by distance from the estimate, z-scores must be monotonically non-decreasing. Generate random estimates, variances, and observation sequences.
- `proptest_health_status_ordering`: Healthy < Degraded < Critical < Hung. For any z-score sequence, the health status must be monotonically non-decreasing with increasing z-score.

## Cross-References
- **wa-2cha** (Mux server watchdog): This bead provides the adaptive threshold intelligence that wa-2cha's watchdog consumes. wa-2cha defines the watchdog architecture (heartbeat registry, health checks, recovery actions), while this bead replaces its fixed thresholds with learned Kalman-filtered thresholds. The z-score health status (Healthy/Degraded/Critical/Hung) maps directly to wa-2cha's recovery action tiers (log/alert/restart component/restart server).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Scalar Kalman filter per component tracking inter-heartbeat intervals
- Adaptive thresholds at mu + k*sigma
- z-score based health status
- Falls back to fixed thresholds during warmup period
- Unit tests: synthetic heartbeat sequences with known anomalies
- Integration test: slow heartbeat detection under simulated load
- Criterion benchmarks meeting latency targets
- Proptest suites for filter convergence and numerical stability","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"WildBeaver","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:04.097844Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T02:00:23.340815-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T02:00:23.340815-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented adaptive watchdog thresholds via Kalman filter. KalmanState in watchdog.rs + KalmanFilter in kalman_watchdog.rs. HealthStatus::Hung added for z>=5. AdaptiveHealthChecker with warmup fallback to fixed thresholds. 52 unit tests, 9 proptests, 4 criterion benchmark groups all under budget.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.7","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:04.097844Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1qz1.8","title":"PIE unit and integration test suite","description":"## Goal
Comprehensive test suite for all Principled Intelligence Engine components: survival model, BOCPD, VOI scheduler, conformal forecasting, Bayesian classifier, continuous backpressure, and adaptive watchdog.

## Test Categories

### 1. Statistical Validation Tests
For each probabilistic component, verify statistical properties:
- Survival model: hazard rate monotonicity, survival function properties (S(0)=1, S(∞)=0)
- BOCPD: detection of known change-points in synthetic data (≥95% detection rate, <5% false alarm)
- Conformal prediction: coverage guarantee (actual coverage ≥ 1-α on held-out data, tested over 1000 runs)
- Bayesian classifier: posterior convergence (with enough evidence, posterior concentrates on true state)
- Kalman filter: estimate converges to true value within 3σ after warmup

### 2. Deterministic Unit Tests
Each component tested with fixed seeds for reproducibility:
- VOI computation: known pane states → verify correct VOI ranking
- Backpressure severity: known queue ratios → verify continuous output
- Evidence ledger: fixed evidence → verify correct log-likelihood contributions

### 3. Integration Tests
- Full pipeline: simulated pane output → BOCPD detects change → classifier updates → VOI reschedules
- Survival + conformal: resource observations → survival model predicts → conformal provides interval → proactive save triggered
- Backpressure + VOI: load spike → severity increases → VOI costs increase → polling frequency drops

### 4. Regression Tests
Golden output files for each component:
- Known input → expected output (sha256 checksums)
- Prevents accidental behavior changes during refactoring

### 5. Performance Benchmarks
Using criterion:
- VOI computation per pane: target < 0.1ms
- BOCPD update per observation: target < 0.05ms
- Bayesian posterior update: target < 0.1ms
- Survival hazard computation: target < 0.01ms
- Conformal interval: target < 1ms

### Implementation Location
- crates/wa-core/tests/pie_unit_tests.rs
- crates/wa-core/tests/pie_integration_tests.rs
- crates/wa-core/benches/pie_benchmarks.rs

### Logging
All tests use structured tracing at DEBUG level:
```rust
#[test]
fn test_bocpd_detects_rate_change() {
    let _guard = tracing_subscriber::fmt()
        .with_max_level(Level::DEBUG)
        .with_test_writer()
        .init();

    // Test body with detailed trace output
}
```

## Dependencies
- Depends on all PIE component beads being implemented
- Can be partially built as each component is completed

## Acceptance Criteria
- 100+ unit tests across all PIE components
- Statistical validation with configurable confidence levels
- Integration tests for cross-component interactions
- Criterion benchmarks meeting performance targets
- Golden output regression tests
- All tests pass with --nocapture showing structured logging","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:05.719543Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-09T21:25:08.841298Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.8","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:05.719543Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.8","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:44.921589Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.8","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:45.05042Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.8","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:45.182651Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.8","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:45.539844Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.8","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:45.660519Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.8","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:45.793479Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.8","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:45.917157Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.8","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1.9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:25:08.687878Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.8","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1.10","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:25:08.841191Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1qz1.9","title":"ADWIN pattern drift detection — alert when agent rules become stale","description":"## Goal
Implement ADWIN (Adaptive Windowing) based pattern drift detection that alerts when agent output patterns change significantly, signaling that regex rules may need updating.

## Background & Motivation
patterns.rs contains 100+ regex rules for detecting agent states (rate limits, errors, prompts). When agents update (new Claude Code version, new Codex release, new Gemini model), their output patterns change. Currently, stale rules silently fail -- no detection, no alert.

ADWIN maintains a variable-length window of observations and detects when the statistical properties of the window change. Applied to pattern detection rates, it catches "this rule used to fire 10 times/day but hasn't fired in 3 days" -- a strong signal the pattern drifted.

## Technical Design

### Per-Rule ADWIN Monitor
For each pattern rule, maintain:
- ADWIN window of detection rates (detections per hour, sampled hourly)
- When ADWIN detects a significant change (drop in detection rate):
  - Emit `pattern.drift` event with rule_id and rate change
  - Suggest fixture capture: "Rule codex.usage.reached may have drifted (0 detections in 72h, was 3.2/day)"

### ADWIN Algorithm (Bifet & Gavalda 2007)
- Maintain variable-length window W of observations
- At each step, test if W can be split into W0 and W1 where:
  |mu(W0) - mu(W1)| >= epsilon_cut (where epsilon_cut depends on window sizes and confidence delta)
- If split found: drop W0 (old data), W1 becomes new window -> DRIFT DETECTED

### Drift Actions
1. **Rate drop detected**: Rule stopped firing
   - Log warning: "Rule X detection rate dropped from Y/day to Z/day"
   - Suggest: "Capture current agent output with: wa robot get-text <pane_id> --tail 500"

2. **Rate spike detected**: Rule firing much more than before
   - Log info: "Rule X detection rate increased from Y/day to Z/day"
   - May indicate: new agent version triggers rule more often (could be false positives)

3. **New unmatched output**: BOCPD detects change-point but no pattern rule fired
   - Strongest signal: agent is doing something new that needs a rule

### Integration with Pattern Engine
- Hook into patterns.rs detection output to feed ADWIN monitors
- One ADWIN instance per rule_id
- Hourly rate sampling (configurable)

### Implementation Location
- New file: crates/wa-core/src/drift.rs
- Integration: patterns.rs detection output feeds drift monitors
- Events: pattern.drift.drop, pattern.drift.spike, pattern.drift.unmatched

## Existing Code References
- patterns.rs: Detection, PatternLibrary (detection rate source)
- events.rs: Event bus (drift event publishing)
- patterns.rs detect_with_context(): detection output to monitor

## Configuration
```toml
[drift]
enabled = true
sample_interval_hours = 1      # Rate sampling frequency
confidence = 0.01              # ADWIN confidence parameter delta
min_history_hours = 48         # Minimum data before drift detection
```

## Dependencies
- Enhanced by BOCPD (change-point + no pattern match = strongest drift signal)
- None required

## Test Requirements

### Criterion Benchmarks
- `adwin_single_observation`: Adding a single observation to an ADWIN window (including split test) must complete in <1us for window sizes up to 1000.
- `adwin_drift_check`: Full drift detection check (window split test with statistical bound computation) must complete in <5us for window size 500.
- `adwin_batch_100_rules`: Updating ADWIN monitors for 100 pattern rules (one observation each) must complete in <100us.
- `adwin_window_resize`: Window shrink operation (dropping old data after drift detection) must complete in <2us.
- Add `[[bench]] name = "adwin_drift" harness = false` to Cargo.toml; use criterion::BenchmarkGroup for the above targets.

### Proptest Specifications
- `proptest_drift_detection_correctness`: For a synthetic rate sequence that changes from mean mu1 to mean mu2 at a known change-point (mu1 in 0.1..10.0, mu2 in 0.1..10.0, |mu1-mu2| > 1.0), ADWIN must detect drift within 2x the window size of the true change-point. Generate sequences of length 100-500.
- `proptest_no_false_drift_stationary`: For a stationary rate sequence (constant mean mu in 0.1..10.0 with Gaussian noise sigma in 0.01..1.0, length 200-1000), ADWIN must not detect drift (false positive rate < 0.05 across 100 random sequences at confidence delta=0.01).
- `proptest_window_invariants`: After any sequence of observations, the ADWIN window size must be >= 1 and the window mean must be within [min(observations), max(observations)].
- `proptest_confidence_monotonicity`: Lower confidence delta must result in fewer drift detections (more conservative). For the same data sequence, delta1 < delta2 implies drift_count(delta1) <= drift_count(delta2).

### LabRuntime DPOR Concurrency Tests
- `concurrent_window_updates`: Using LabRuntime with DPOR exploration, verify that multiple threads updating the same ADWIN window concurrently (simulating concurrent pattern detection events from multiple panes) produce a valid window state. Model 3 concurrent updaters with 10 observations each, assert window invariants (size, mean, no data corruption) hold under all interleavings.
- `concurrent_drift_and_pattern_detection`: Verify that ADWIN drift checks running concurrently with new pattern detections being recorded do not miss drift events or produce spurious detections. Model interleaved observe() and check_drift() calls from 2 threads.

## Cross-References
- **wa-1qz1.2** (BOCPD): ADWIN and BOCPD are complementary drift detectors. BOCPD detects statistical change-points in raw output metrics (entropy, rate), while ADWIN monitors detection rate changes in the pattern engine's output. The strongest drift signal occurs when BOCPD detects a change-point AND ADWIN shows a rate drop for a specific rule -- this means agent behavior changed AND the pattern engine's coverage degraded simultaneously.
- **wa-283h4.9** (Spectral fingerprinting): When ADWIN detects pattern drift, the spectral fingerprinter can be triggered to recompute the frequency spectrum of the affected pane's output. This helps distinguish between "pattern drifted because agent changed" vs "pattern drifted because workload changed" by comparing frequency signatures before and after the drift point.

## Acceptance Criteria
- ADWIN monitor per pattern rule tracking detection rates
- Drift detection when rate drops or spikes significantly
- Alert events with actionable suggestions
- Integration with pattern engine detection output
- Unit tests: synthetic rate sequences with known drift points
- Integration test: simulate rule becoming stale, verify alert
- Criterion benchmarks meeting latency targets
- Proptest suites for drift detection correctness
- LabRuntime DPOR tests for concurrent window update safety","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"WildBeaver","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:25.270917Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:48:25.322879-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T01:48:25.322879-05:00","close_reason":"ADWIN drift detection implemented: variable-length window with Hoeffding bound, per-rule monitors, drift events with suggestions, multi-rule DriftMonitor. 32 tests passing.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.9","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:24:25.270917Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-1qz1.9","depends_on_id":"wa-3cyp","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-09T21:25:08.412551Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-1s9k","title":"FTUI-06.4 Migrate filter and search input widgets with robust editing semantics","description":"## Background\nText input regressions in filters/search dramatically degrade operator workflow quality.\n\n## Deliverables\n- reusable text input primitives for filters/search\n- cursor/editing behavior parity (backspace, movement, clear, focus transitions)\n- view integration for panes/events/search surfaces\n\n## Acceptance Criteria\n- editing semantics are deterministic and tested\n- filter/search interactions remain fast and predictable.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LavenderGrove","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:08:30.688930506Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor","updated_at":"2026-02-09T03:32:03.902648633Z","closed_at":"2026-02-09T03:32:03.90257183Z","close_reason":"done","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1s9k","depends_on_id":"wa-2zd7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:08:30.701202909Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-1s9k","depends_on_id":"wa-2h1j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:19:54.977870372Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"},{"issue_id":"wa-1s9k","depends_on_id":"wa-3any","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-08T20:20:04.536545082Z","created_by":"GrayHarbor"}]}
